speaker: interviewer
text: I'm here with Dr. Roden. Dr. Roden, you were just telling me about your arrival in Birkenau-Auschwitz, and how you bumped into Eva in the washroom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was a prearranged meeting. And I succeeded to stay there about five or 10 minutes after the curfew. Theoretically, I was permitted to do so, for I was a servant to the head of the block. But the second in charge, his name was Nathan, N-A-T-H-A-N, the first name was John. He was second in charge. And he caught me. And he said, there are no exceptions to the rule. And you are not the exception to the rule. And you'll get 25 hits. And he took a cane. I put my head into the chimney, and I got 25 hits. In fact, he broke my L2, my second lumbar vertebra, which I found out many, many years later. It was quite something, for I couldn't move for a couple of days properly. And I've got a few there still on my skin. But it was the only, only time when I was beaten while spending the whole time under Nazi regime. For I was lucky beyond description. The head of the camp, his name was John Bondy, written with a B-O-N-D-Y. When we came, when we arrived-- so a few hours later, we have been-- no, it was the next day. The next day, we have been standing in rows and waiting for being tattooed. Every prisoner got a serious tattoo number. I was standing just behind my father, and behind me was standing my father-in-law. So it was rather funny, for we had numbers, consecutive numbers. People always ask the question if they'd hurt, or whether it was something-- I know, this is a quite secondary thing, which is done with a special pen. And that went, then, to a central registry. And in the central registry many months later, I found out that in our-- on each of our names is written, arrived [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then and then. And our date was 18th of July, 1944. SB, was written behind everybody's-- from our transport-- number, after everybody's-- so that month's, 18th, so July Sonderbehandelung, special treatment, means gas.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had arrived in Auschwitz in late December 1943.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was immediately decided--

speaker: interviewee
text: Always-- every-- every this kind of showpiece should have lasted six months. For they were expecting that somebody will arrive from, again, from the International Red Cross, and that they will bamboozle them this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know the date that was written on your ID card?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I-- I can mention it later-- how I got to it, how I got to the central registry.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The normal-- so I was working there as a-- cleaning in the block and bringing whatever was necessary, or whatever was asked from me. And I was a kind of a connecting liaison between the blocks. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Men in the camp were either doing nothing, simply just waiting and waiting. And everybody who was capable to work, or of work, was taken, forming special commandos, and taken outside of the camp to a quarry, where they had been carrying stones into the camp. The camp-- we want us to imagine they are the-- our compound of Auschwitz Zwei, or Birkenau, consisted of two huge sets of camps. The third one they were just building. They were camps which were about a kilometer long, and about 300 yards wide, surrounded by barbed wire attached to each other-- about six of them in a row. Then through the middle went-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the year 1943, already, they were finishing it-- was at a rail track going through the main gate. On the main gate was the famous, 'Arbeit Macht Frei,' 'You will be free through work.' And that went to the end where the train stopped normally. The people were thrown out, went, then, in columns through-- they said, go forward, forward, forward. They were standing, the SS men, who were assaulting them and similar, during the selection. We were spared. We stayed in this camp, which was on the left side. The second one, it was a compound. The block called was B. And it was B2B. That was our camp. And they were 16-- these huge blocks on each side. And in front, in the entrance into the camp, there was a kitchen. And then there was, I mean, some kind of a central-- some kind of registry for the camp. And then there were a block where people were living. Female-- women lived on one side, men on the other side. And on the top block on the-- closer to the railroad there, there was a children's block. And the width between the blocks was about 100 meters. And twice a day, in the morning, and in the evening, we were called to something called appell-- A-P-P-L-L, which was the counting. We were supposed to stand five abreast-- so that the SS men who came into the block, and was counting, had a slate with a chalk. And he would just make for every row, he would make a line. And every fifth line, he would cross. It was some kind of-- their simplified method. I suspect that some of them didn't know, probably, how to count. The-- and this went twice a day. And it was raining most of the time. And the whole terrain was soft, was packed kind of earth. And it became muddy so much so that, I mean, within the half an hour or an hour, depending what they-- what kind of nonsense they wanted to announce, or wanted to do, or just to punish us, we sunk at least, I don't know, up until your ankles, or sometimes even deeper into the mud. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The food which we got depended, again, in what position we would be in. So being a servant to the-- to the head of the block, I got the special ration, probably a double ration, which was usually a kind of a soup which was prepared in the kitchen. And the soup consisted mostly of water. And there was something added to it. The favorite addition was ersatz, a replacement of something. And then there was a-- the meal consisted mostly of turnips. And the turnips, were most of the time, were a little bit rotten, and so that for years, I couldn't even smell turnip. And occasionally, I mean, we might get a potato, or something, like that, depending simply on the SS man who was in charge, or whoever was in charge, had a better kind of food supply or better mood. And then we had some kind of a coffee, which was done out coffee ersatz. Altogether, calorie-wise, we got approximately 800 calories, while the rest of Birkenau didn't get more than 500. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people were dying in the camp without any kind of interference. The corpses were always taken to the back of the block. And every morning they were carried forward. And there came a special commando with hay wagons. And they were thrown on the hay wagons, and 14 men were dragging this hay wagon towards the crematorium. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The daily mortality was approximately between 200 and 500-- 200, 300.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your block? Are you referring to--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp, in the B2B camp. This-- I was always capable and able to give something, get something to Eva, who lived in a different block, or to my or Eva's parents. I succeeded to shield both of the father and father-in-law, that they didn't have to work. Eva worked with children in the children's block, which was, again-- it wasn't a Potemkin's village. It was a double Potemkin's village. And then one day, I proclaimed to somebody that I am trained as a male nurse. And through a fluke, I got myself to the hospital block. There were three blocks which were hospital compounds. And I was working as a male orderly, or a nurse. They took everybody who was sick and put him there. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the kind of German preciseness, or the German kind of absurd punctuality, there were people who were waiting for their death. And they were dying like flies. They had to get special kinds of ration, and special kind of bread. So they were getting white bread instead of the bread which we were-- sorry, we got also, each one of us, a piece of bread every day, which was a dark, military bread, which was not bad. I still somehow like the bread-- for it has got a certain kind of trace of memory.

speaker: interviewee
text: So these sick people, who are just this sick, simply they got special kind of white, piece of white bread, which is not unlike like a big, bloated kind of the white French bread we have got here. It was divided then, depending, again, on the number of the sick persons and the number-- and the kind of mood of whoever was cutting it. When did you begin working in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: I began working about six weeks after-- after we arrived. Maybe months-- six weeks after I arrived. And then, in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] June 6, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1944-- no, no, no, no, no. What am I saying? They got off in-- in March 6, 1944. An order came that the original transport, which came there, the 5,000 people from Theresienstadt, they will be transferred to a different camp called, Heydebreck, H-E-Y-D-E-B-R-E-C-K. And every truck-- such a move of populace in any camp, everybody knows that it probably is deadly. And the transport was supposed to leave on the 6 of March. On 6 of March, each and every block was completely blocked. There were special kind of squadron of SS men was put into the camp. There were several hundred SS men with dogs and with guns. And they were transferred. These 5,000 people they transferred to the camp A, neighboring to us. Prepared for transportation. By the same time, we had such a kind-- such kind there was a certain resistance in-- in the camp. And from camp to camp went a signal that the special commando which handled-- handles the gas chambers and crematoria is called 'the full attention.' And at the same time, there is a special amount of SS men guarding the truck toward-- towards the-- towards the gas chambers. And during the night, simply, the blocks were closed. There was absolute [GERMAN], closure of the camp. And we heard terrible noises, and terrible beating, and terrible screams. And they were loading them into trucks naked. They knew perfectly well where they are going. And they took all 5,000 people. And literally, without missing a night, they gassed them. 22 people were pulled out of transport. And they were the personnel which were nursing personnel, and few doctors, who were pulled out by the chief angel of death called Dr. Josef Mengele. Also, about six young boys were brought back, or brought into the-- in that case, into the camp of the-- where the adult males were. 28 people were pulled out by Mengele. And we had an order that we should prepare everybody who is in the hospital compound for transportation. And we should prepare them on stretchers for carrying them onto the trucks. They didn't have any stretchers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So from the bunks, we ripped off the-- kind of one plank on each side. And we took a blanket, and in front of the blanket, we put four knots on it, and were prepared to put the sick people on it.

speaker: interviewee
text: While we were working with it, it was already early in the morning. And suddenly, the door opens into the block. And in walks a slew of SS men, headed with Mengele, and headed with [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] the head of Auschwitz. I forgot who it was at that point, the head of Auschwitz. I think that it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know whether it was Herr-- I forgot. But they changed. And anyhow, now, the normal routine was that there was a guard in front of the block who should warn that an SS man, in this case, I mean, the whole SS hierarchy is approaching. And the-- the man responsible, the block Eltester, the head of the block, had to prepare everybody into attention and report, there are so and so many, and so and so many prisoners. And this-- this was not done, for nobody was there. Everybody left with the transport. And I was talking about luck or no luck, I was the first one who was there, doing the knots on the-- on this so-called stretcher.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I stood into attention and clicked heels. And I reported that prisoner number 10065765 reports, block number 32 with so and so many prisoners, so and so many prisoners sick, so and so many prisoners died, so and so many prisoners working at preparing the stretchers. He says, what? I says, preparing the stretchers according to the ordinance number so and so which we got in the morning. And the SS man looked at me and said to one of these lower rank SS men, take his number. And he took my number.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we were standing, then, in the morning at the appell-- so we somehow managed to arrange ourselves to five. And suddenly a number was called from the SS barracks, which were, I mean, down outside of the camp, one kilometer from the-- far from the place I was. And for the third time, they called the number. And I realized it's my number.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I head-- I ran down. Now, you couldn't walk. For [GERMAN], 'A prisoner moves while jogging.' For years, I couldn't understand why I cannot jog. And then it dawned upon me. So I jogged down, and then the SS man simply said to another guy, the other SS man there, gives it to him. So I don't know whether I will get it-- but I got a small roll, a kind of tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I, again, jogged back to it. And there I unrolled it. And I realized that I became the head of the hospital compounds at the age of 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the SS officer who gave you this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Dr. Josef Mengele. He said, take his number. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And Mengele had there-- in our block were the twins, their project with twins. He was gathering twins, trying to do all kinds of measurements and all kinds of questions and all kinds of tests. Luckily, they didn't do any vivisections of them. It didn't go so far. For I am sure that he wanted to find a method by which you can multiply the birth, so that, if already a German woman gets pregnant, it should be worthwhile.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Roden, when you refer to the hospital, can you try to pinpoint for us where exactly that was located, and walk around the exterior for us to describe what you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was the rail track-- the train-- train track. And there was a space of about 50 meters, where the people who were thrown out of the-- came off with the train, were thrown out. And then there was, I mean, a barrier of barbed wire along the camps. Then there was about 50 meters where there was a block, which was a latrine. And then the next block was block number 32, then there was block number 30, block number 28. This was the hospital compound, these three blocks. Then they were only two blocks. Then there was only one block for people who were dying. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] outside was nothing. I'm lying-- actually, in the spring, on the other side, there was-- the first barrack was the so-called wash-- washing facilities. The next block was the children's block. And when I walked around the washing facilities, being, I mean, having the band on my arm and similar, and reporting to Mengele every day personally, so I had the privileged position that I could move wherever. And there behind the block, in the spring suddenly, I have seen a small tree growing up. And the tree had green leaves. It sounds idiotic, but somehow, looking at the tree, I had a notion, if this tree can survive, so can you. If this tree made it, maybe you'll make it, too. It was one of these things-- that was the only thing which was growing there. And in the position as I was, every day I reported to the SS man who was on duty for-- there were so-called sick people. It was a conglomerate of diseases starting for us-- pneumonia, ending with typhus, and typhoid fever, and special dysentery which, I think, they spread among us. It was, what I found out many, many years later, a dysentery called Shiga-Kruse-- which was prevalent-- which is prevalent in [NON-ENGLISH]. They're testing it for the army. And people are dying. And people were lying there every morning. And my duty was to also write the death certificate for these dead people, for they had to be taken away, taken off the central registry. And so I had to take the tattoo number. It was written on a special form. There were two diagnoses-- ONE diagnosis was pneumonia, THE other one was weakness of the heart. And that was up to my choice, simply, what I put there. And I had to sign it, 10065765. So I-- many, many times a day I was signing this thing. Then I had a very unpleasant duty-- that is, from all the corpses, we had to take our golden teeth. That means when the body's stiff, you had to use a crowbar to open. We had the special commando which pulled out the teeth, and collect it in a sack. And every morning, it was given to the SS man on duty-- probably, I mean, promptly stole it. But the-- that was-- and then came Mengele, and asked all kinds of questions. And I-- I had to explain it to him, just in one single sentences. In my presence, he never beat or never hit or never do anything for that it was-- he was probably too high here, delegated his responsibility to the lower-- lower kinds of ranks-- the Germans, usually from the occupied territories somewhere along the Danube, or from former Yugoslavia, or from Romania, or from Hungary. His German-- their German was extremely poor, but they succeeded.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe for me Dr. Mengele?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a relatively tall, good-looking man, who had a stony face. Didn't smile, didn't laugh, didn't make a grimace, didn't do anything. Also, when I have seen him-- and I have seen him on the ramp-- where he went for a few hours, probably, to entertain himself-- being present at the selection of the people who were coming, sending them either to camp that was about every 10th, every 20th body which was capable of some work, or to their destruction. Some people asked me if I have really seen him, personally. Well, I can confirm that he was there. I was away from him a maximum of 50 meters. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And as such, sometimes we were ordered to bring into the camp some supplies.

speaker: interviewer
text: I am about to finish the tape. It's OK. Continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got the hay wagon with the 14 men. We pulled out. We reported to the SS men at the gate, who simply took our numbers. And we were sent either to the female camp for blankets, which were repaired there, or we were sent for supplies, medical supplies, or from the oncoming train that we-- I did altogether three times.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a harrowing experience. For we were mingled directly with the people who are coming for the selection. And we were always interfering. Who says that we wouldn't be passed through the same thing? But it did not happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, on three occasions, we were sent to something, part of the camp called Kanada. Why they called it Kanada, nobody knows. But probably it was the magnificent Canadian joke, namely, all the merchandise-- everything what came with the transport, which was seized from the prisoners, came to this barracks-- to these barracks. There were about nine of them, and they were sorted, to-- there were thousands of glasses and watches and everything, all in the huge, huge kind of collections.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Roden, we're going to take a break right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
